CURRERT AFFAIKy,

- Wo have received a list of the successful candidates
for the five science and technological scholarships, and
for the five teacherships-in-training at the Royal College
of Science, Dublin, offered for open competition by the

- Department.  The list has set us thinking; to some
xtent it has perplexed us. The scholarships are value
or £50 a year for three years, entitling the holder to

 free instruction during the Associate Course, and third-
lass railway fare for one journey, each session, to and
Dublin. A teachership-in-training entitles the
o free instruction during the Associato Course;
ntenance allowance of 21s. a week for tho session
forty weeks each year ; a third-class railway fare

journey, each session, to and from Dublin.

Now, all tho five scholarships were won by pupils
named, respectively, Joughim, Elmes, Fletcher, Craw-
ford and Shankey, out of Mountjoy School, a “ Saved '’
school in Dublin. Of the five teacherships-in-training
two were won by Messrs. Moffatt and Wookey, of Mount-
joy School, . Dublin; one by a W. D. Haigh, of the
Christian Brothers, Cork; one by a Joshua W. Smith,
of Strabane,-and the remaining one by Rev. Thomas F.
Molloy, of St. Patrick’s College, Maynooth.

All of the five £50 a year -for three years scholar-
ships were won by students of Mountjoy School. Of
the five teacherships-in-training two went to the same
school. What are the Catholic schools doing? Last
yoar four of the five scholarships were won seemingly
by Protestants, one of whom, however, was educated by
the Christian Brothers of Clonmel. The fifth scholar-
ship was won by a student, probably a Catholic, of the
Christian Brothers, Thurles. Of the five teacherships-
in-training given last year three appear to have been
won by Catholic boys, a fourth by a Protestant taught
by the Christian Brothers, Clonmel, and the fifth by
one Thomas Haigh, from the Christian Brothers, Cork.

How is it that Mountjoy School has scored so heavily
this year? A number of Catholic schools and colleges
earn more under the Intermediate than Mountjoy School
earns.  Are the Catholic schools overlooking these
scholarships? It certainly looks curious that all five of
the valuable scholarships should have gone to students
of Mountjoy School. Is it that Seoinin students of
the shop-keeping and farming classes will not see beyond
tho genteel circle of doctors, lawyers, and the rest, and
leave almost a walk-over to Mountjoy School for valu
able science and technological scholarships!

A few years ago a friend of ours had a conversation
with the head * of a big and popular
Catholic  school; the latter was dilating upon
the mumber of Intermediate prizes  which
had been won. Our friend was, no doubt, impressed,
but the information he particularly wanted was con-
cerning the permanent prizes or positions which the
students of the school had secured in the world. The
head of the school admitted frankly and regretfully that
he could point to little but petty clerkships.

The Horse Show came off in the grounds of “ The
Eldest Bigot of them all,”” otherwise the Royal Dublin
Society. This is a very exclusive society so far as its
paid staff is concerned. Wo believe it is even more
“Saved ”’ than the notorious Rathmines District
Council of the 10s. 6d. in the £ rates. One Horse Show
is vory much like another Horse Show It is a really
important function for those who deal in horse flesh,
but as horse flesh is outside owr purview we look at the
Show chicfly as a peep-show, in which capacity it in-
terests most of the people who go there.

Many of our readers will remember that we took a
stand at the Horse Show of 1901. We explained at
the time that if we were in the Horse Show we were not
of it. We wrote at the time, almost exactly five years
ago: —* There will not be much Irish there, though our
sign-board will have Sinn Fém upon it, and, no
doubt, many a genteel missy from the villages will
giggle before it, and say— How awfully funny;’ and
many a nation-killer will feel a qualm of conscience.
We don’t like paying the un-Irish Royal Dublin Society
the cost of our modest stand, but they bar the way
with their tariffs, and it serves our turn to go in there.

34

Therefore we go in—mildly laughing at it before wo
enter.”’ £

The Horse Show is not now much of a business thing
except in the way of live stock, and so we were not im-
pressed much by the complaint of a friend of ours con-
cerning the lack of Irish industrial exhibits in the
gallery. Foxford tweeds were in great array in their usual
corner, and for the first timo Dripsey cloths made their
appearance. Amongst the cloths shown at the Dripsey
stand was a safiron-tinted stuff for Irish national cos-
tumes. A verb “to toy '’ show was held in the Indus-
trial Hall, the usual sort of luxuriecs were on show, and
the usual sort of people dawdled round ; and that is all
we have to say about it.

In the gallery the smutoscopes were, as usual, to the
-fore appealing by suggestive titles to the prurient
visitors. We are almost tired protesting against these
smutty catch-pennies. We know nothing of them
beyond the titles that are put on the outside, and if
what you see for your penny is not smutty, then the
pennies are collected—so far as some of the pictures are
concerned—on false pretences.

Amongst the members of the Council of this Royal
Dublin Society that rents out space every year to these
smutozcopes are James Talbot Power, Joseph R.
O’Reilly, D.L.; Rt. Rev. Mgr. Molloy, D.D., Sc.D.;
Professor Barrett, Professor J. M. Purser, Sir James
Cre=d Meredith, Rev. Canon F. F. Carmichael, M.A.,
LL.D.; Joseph M‘Grath, LL.D. Why do not some of
these protest against the Society exploiting smutoscopes?
And 1f any of them have protested, and protested in
vain, why does not the particular member of the Council
resign by way of public protest against the Royal
Dublin Society trafficking in smutty pictures? Surely
the Rt. Rev. Mgr. Molloy, Rector of the Catholic Uni-
versity and Castle diner-out, ought to take action. ' It
is scandalous to have these smutoscopes appealing yearly
to the pennies of the prurient, and yet apparently no
member of the Council makes a protest

Seoinin Dublin kicked up its heels in great style
because some fow ships of the Atlantic Fleet—which pro-
vide one “ Idolatrous’’ priest for the 2,000, or there-
abouts, Catholics on the yarious boats, and the hospital
at Gibraltar—deigned to visit Kingstown. There was
a ball, and we supposs lots of the women folk of “tony’”
Bung flocked there in the 'opes of ’aving a dawnse with
a naval hofficer. We saw some trams passing by Lans-
downe Road on the night of the ball, and several of them
contained female couples without a male escort, done
up in regulation ball-going style. ~ Shoneenery must,
indeed, be strong in women who go in a tram to a public
ball by themselves. It is often those sort of people who
have not a male escort, nor the price of a cab, who put
on most “side’” in the tram-cars, We would not be sur-
prised if the conversation of these women did not descend
beneath tlis rank of vice-admiral whilst there were any
fellow-passengers in the trams to listen to them.

Most of our readers have, no doubt, read that recently
a momorial to Father O’Neill, who was flogged by tho
represontatives of “ civic virtue,” was recently unveiled
at Youghal. &n tatan Peaoap, Canon O'Leary,
made a stirring speech in Irish on tho occasion.  The
memory of the flogged priest, Father O’Neill, is not the
‘only one honoured by the memorial, the memory of
others who suffered in’98 at the hands of British “civic
virtue”’ is also perpetuated.  Charles Gallagher and
Charles O’Bricn, United Irishmen, who wero hanged
by the custodians of civic virtue,” and Patrick
M‘Carthy, a blacksmith, who was flogged from tho shafts
of his own cart because he would not reveal the names
of men who had given him orders for pikes, are also
commemorated.

The Freemason gang in Youghal and their hangers-
on would rather that these little by-gone samples of
‘“civic virtue ’’ were not commemorated at all  When

it was proposed to erect the memorial _i'u the public
park, the ““Saved” members of the Town (,,: uncil
strenuously opposed the propt_)sa,l.‘ The Savcd‘ were
backed up by some Cawtholics,”” Messrs. R. Cary, M,

Tho ercction of the memorial in the park to British
“ ¢ivic virtue’’ and Irish martyrs was only, postponed,
howover. Last year the Cm.'ﬂnu‘l,tec appealed to the
olectors to give them a majority in favour of the ercc-
tion of the memorial, and the electors did so. The
anti-Trish gang and their hangors-on started on new
tact A leaflet was issued informing Lh.c public that,
it was illegal to demand a charge for entering the park ;
another leaflet advised the public not to enter the park
on the occasion of the unveiling of the memorial
becauso Father O’Neill was a traitor, and the promoters
of the memorial to his memory were actuated solely
by a desire to arouse and perpetuate religious dissension
in the town! However, these leaflets did not appear to
have much effect ; the funds for the memorial still came
volling in. Where leaflets failed perhaps 'tactics
similar to the Trinity College mud-throwers might be
effective.  On the morning of the 6th August the
pedestal of the memorial in the park was discovered
daubed all over with tar. Leaflets and tar are the
weapons of the Youghal Sourfaces against the memory
of Trishmen who, in the ’'98 period, suffered from the
“civic virtue'’ of British man-floggers. We have no
doubt that the fellows that tarred the pedestal would
be happier if they were flogging Canon Keller as Father
O’Neill was flogged by the instruments of “civic
virtue’’ over a hundred years ago. The Sourfaces
must have felt some of the molten tar rising in their
own insides when they found that the funds for the
memorial flowed in more freely than ever after their
brilliant charge with tar brushes on the pedsstal.

On the 7th August the cutrage was properly denounced
at a meeting of the local branch of the United Irish
League. The report of the meeting and the resolution
passed appeared in the Cork Constitution, the Unionist
paper; the “only national ”” of Cork characteristically
enough refused to print the resolution. A representa-
tive of the “only national,”” on being questioned about
the omission, explained that the remarks of the proposer
were too strong! They weren’t too strong for the
Unionist paper! Strange, is it not? However, we do
not suppose that our Cork readers would consider
strange any manifestation of lack of backbone on the
part of the “only national,”’ the Cork Ezaminer. The
representative of the “only national’”  was asked
why the resolution was not published, but the fountain
of excuses which rise in the ingenious minds of such
persons as “only national ” representatives had evi-
dently run dry, and no answer was forthcoming. Sub-
sequently the “only national ’ representative, on being
taunted with the fact that the Unionist Constitution
had published what was omitted by the so-called Na-
tionalist paper, blurted out that some person, or per-
sons, from Youghal had been to the Zaraminer office,
and had intimidated the “only national.””  Perhaps
thero was a threat made to flog Mr. Crosbie or tar the
manager |

‘What do the Sourfaces of Youghal intend to do now
that the memorial has been unveiled, and stands mutely
teaching ils lesson to those who have enough intelli-
gence to receive it?  Many of the frequenters of the
weekly band promenade during the season would
doubtless like to cutrage the memori A memorial
that ab once symbolisos Irvish opposition to British
despotism in '98, and that particularly perpetuates the
memory of an Irish priest standing high up in the centre
of tho park where the cream— Saved ”’ and * tame "’'—
of Youghal’s social grandeur deploys on band evenings
and other important occasions must bo boiling tar to the -
clique of Freemasons in the district. Round the Free- -
mason orb of Youghal a few more than “ tame ' Catho-

if they were given the chance they would not adopt the
samo tactics in vegard to the government of the coun-

try?’  Warming up to his work this “ Sir Knight and
Brother ™ declared :—* The time had arrived when Pro-
testants should bestir themselves. Lot them bo

animated by the same resolute spirit displayed by the
men who defended Derry’s Walls.  Lef them arouse
enthusiasm among Protestants and’ Unionists through-
out the length and breadth of the land, for the timo was
surely coming when they would all have to take their
stand against those who wero for the disruption and
dismemberment of the cmpire.  Their cry, as cver,
would bs ¢ No Surrender.’ *’

A “strong ™ resolution was then proposed by “Sir
Knight and Brother *’ Francis C. Johnston, who gavo it
“hot** to Sir Antony MacDonnell, and the resolution
was seconded by “ Sir Knight and Brother * C. J. Alli-
«son, who wagged his tongue excitedly at “ Rome.”’

Evidently the City of Belfast Grand Black Chapter is
a sort of academy for mutual conferring of “Sir Knight-
hoss,” for all 't} 2r5 appearsd to Lo
Knight.””  “ Sir Knight and Brother Edward ”” Dougan
wound up his harangue with this excellent specimen of
Orange raimeis:

Was it not time for those who had means and leisure to go out,
or send out, efficient missionaries across tho channel to cducate
the huge masses of electors there, who were moustrously ignorant
of Irish affairs, as to the real situation? It would surcly be
criminal to neglect what might turn out to be the last, and yet
the best opportunity of saving the empi If commercial Ireland
permitted itself to be taxed out of existenco by Mr. Redmond;
if Prote:tant Ireland permitted itself to Le hustled out of all employ-
ment by the “Roman Catholic Association” and “ Gaelic League;”
if Belfast trade unionists accepted a leader who took, lying down,
“The Pope” as a toast when King Edward VIL was ignored, ho
thought whatever suffering and wrong the future might bring to
them, the verdict of posterity would be ‘“serve. them right.” But,
as he beliecved and hoped would be the case, they would play the
part of men, struggling, fighting, aye, and if nced be, dying like
men; and the sacrifices would redound to the benefit of their
children and those of unborn generations, who, thinking of the
race, tho old Imperial race, from whence they sprang, would
follow in the footsteps of deathless siros.”

S

We wonder what sort of people are “ deathless sives?’
This is the sort of stuff that is ladled out to the poor
ignorant Orange dupes by ranters of these “Sir

" Rnight *’ pattern, and it is well to give our readers

samples of it from time to time.

Tho Great Western Railway of England have issued
an advertisement of the Rosslare route. It is bi-lin-
gual—English and French? No. English and Ger-
man? No. Why, it is enough to make Brother Gould-
ing, “gontleman,”” throw himself down one of his own
manure factory chimneys to find the Great Western
of England—England of which he is ono of the faithful
guardians—advertising in English and Z7ish. We
recall the gencral meeting at the Great Sourface Rail~

way at Kingsbridge when the question of Irish as an .

optional subject for the examination for clerks was
raised. The manifestation of the anti-Irish virus of
the Sourfaces at that meeting was almost a revelation
to us who may have been expected to have measured the
Sourface to his lowest depth. The Sourfaces at that
meeting almost frothed at the mouth with anti-Irish
rage. Andmow the Great Western Railway of England
is advertising the Rosslare route in Zrish, and not only
doing it in papers in Ireland, but they did it in the
London Daily Mail. But then these English railway
directors are perhaps only triflers and sentimentalists
compared with such lords of commz:ce as Pilkington, of

Under the Leading ““Feir an Ceicinnog-Cluain-
meala,”” we have received the following : —“ As adjudi-
cators in the language competitions at the above fFeip
we ask for a small space in your columns for a few
remarks thereon. The members of the Clonmel Branch
who presented themselves in reading, conversation, dic-
tation and letter-writing were all that could be desired.
They possess a great grip of the language, and, best of
all, they speak it well. They need never speak a word
of English in their conversation with one another hence-
forward.  The branch can justly be proud of them.
The pity is that there are so few of them. They are
the material from which the Irish nation is to be
revived. It is a matter for deep regret that none of the
schools sent forward competitors. The shield presented
by Mr. T. Morrissey, ex-Mayor, is being allowed to rusb
for want of competition. Have the parents in Clonmel
made an honest demand to have Irish properly taught
their children, or do they show any interest in what their
children ought to learn at all? It is disheartening to us
not to find the youth of the town entering in healthy
rivalty in the language competitions.  Either the
parents or the teachers are to blame, or both perhaps.
Thers is no use in blaming the children. In this respect
the peip was very discouraging.—pPaopnis O Cadly,

friars of the anti-Catholic imagination usually stamp
upon “friars’’ contemplating drink. Wo read under
the picture, “ I'm clothed in sack-cloth for my sin, With
old sack twine I'm lined within.”

This is a curious country, and in this military and
Anglicised town of Cahir, in “Gallant’’ Tip., theso cards
were picked up in a shop run by a Catholic! They were
* exposed publicly for sale on top of a case of Catholic
prayer-books ! Scapﬁlms hung from the ceiling just above
them! The young woman in attendance, while admiffing
to one who raised the question that an apology was duc
for exposing them for sale, attempted to excuso herself
by asserting that the traveller for the firm that issued
these foreign post-cards would not take them back, as
thoy sold everywhere! It appears that this shopkeepor,
of Cahir, did not order these post-cards, but they wero
sent to him, and he paid for them! This is protty busi-
ness, indeed. So a traveller in pictorial insults to
Catholics can dump his goods on a shop run by a Catho-
lic in Cahir, in Gallant Tip., and refuse to take them
back. The backbone of somo of our Idolatrous’’
people is certainly prodigious.

A correspondent in a recent number of the LEApER
drew attention to the evils of drink at threshings. IHis
Lordship the Bishop of Killaloe has taken the cvil by
the horus, and has issued a pastoral letter which was
read in all the churches in his diocese. In the course of
= his pastoral, Dr. Fogarty writes: —

A few customs, however, still survive as painful relics of a
malignant disease not yet entirely cured. One of these is tho
distribution of drink at the threshings in the hacrvest, an abuse
which is of recent growth, and is peculiar (o cortain districts or
parishes, but which. wherever it exists, becomes a fruitful source
of drunkenness and demoralisation, and not unfrequently of fatal
accidents around the machines, It is a senseless and wicked waste
of money, and one of its worst effects is that it helps to perpetuate
in the minds of the growing generation the detestable superstition
that the giving of drink is the sign or a proof of cither respect-
ability or generosity. I appeal to the good =ense of the people to
brush aside false human respect in matters of this kind, and to
take concerted action with our pricsts to stamp out, once and for
all, this barbarous custom. We, therefore, direct that in every
parish or district where the abuse exists a meeting be held after
Mass on the first Sunday in September and combined action agreed
upon that no farmer or employer will give intoxicating drink of
any kind at gatherings of this kind in the future. If it is friends
who come to help us, let them receive a hospitality worthy of

friends and of Christians, but no drink; and if it be workingmen
I& who are employed, let them get a decent and honest wage; but

the labourer is mean and unworthy of sympathy who expects, and
is prepared to take drink on these occasions. The labouring man
has his character to maintain and his self-respect, which is as
dear to him as that of tho richest man on the land, and he is
entirely forgetful of his‘own interests if he fosters in any way
a scandalous habit which degrades and injures him.

Meetings have since been held in accordance with the

B directions of Dr. Fogarty. The Very Rev. Dean White,
I3 P.P., of Nenagh, presided at a meeting of farmers, at
[ which it was resolved to fall in with Dr. Fogarty’s
advice; at other meetings also similar resolutions were

arrived at. All this may be called dealing with the

drink evil by the direct method. The country would
be the gainer by an extension of the method.

Some TIrishmen in the East End of TLondon are
anxious to show evidence of their appreciation of the
services of Father Twomey to the cause of the Trish
Language and National movement by presenting him
with a token of their regard. Father Twomey has been

EgBRITISH JOURNALISM AND.ITS IRISH TAIL,

ANY of the trades societies in Ireland are hitched
on to British trades unions. Ireland that is
yearning for the power to manage its own legislative
and administrative affairs is, so far as many of its
trades, including the journalistic trade, are concerned,
a mora tail tacked on to Britain. The members of the
British Institute of Journalists are in conference in
West Britain as we write, and many of our ““ Nationa-
list *’ journalists who could govern Ireland with their
quills—if they had Home Rule—are evidently not equal
to running a journalists’ combine in Ireland and for
Ireland. The heart of the difficulty is that there are'
very few Irish journalists—most of the journalists in
Ireland are West Britons.

We are not now concerned with the British Institute
of Journalists as a business or professional combine; we
are only interested in it from the Irish point of view.
From the Irish point of view the humiliating fact is
that a large number of journalists in Ireland, including
many on the staff of the Freeman’s Journal, are con-
tent to be an insignificant tail stuck on to a body that
has its head in London. We have not read very much
about the doings of the British journalists. We believe
there was a garden party at the Lord Lieutenant’s
Lodge, and junior West British reporters, no doubt,
felt exalted as they trod viceregal daisies and lawn grass
under their feet. The Reception Committee of the
Dublin Conference gave a musical ‘“ At Home
on Saturday. The guests were received by, amongst
others, James Carlyle, a Scotchman, we understand, who
was imported by the “ ZIrish’’ Tmes, and Mr. W.
Algernon Locker, another imported Britisher, who is, or
was, editor of that paper; he was certainly editor of it
at the time the paper foully libelled Canon M‘Inerney,
a base and impudent libel for which Arnott’s paper had
to pay heavily. Needless to say, we do not read that
there was any mere Irish song on the programme, but
a Tommy Atkins band was present.

Tho fact that Trinity College gave a welcome to the
British Institute of Journalists is sufficient evidence of
the class of corporation it is. Indeed, the Examina-
tion Hall of the notorious Spelling Bee Institution was
placed at the disposal of the British and West British
journalists for the transaction of business, and Anthony
Traill, who would be known in the world of letters if
his ability bore any considerable proportion to his anti-
TIrish bigotry, took the chair. The Provost wore aca-
demic costume whilst in the chair, and we certainly
think hoe acted shrewdly in doing so, for it was well to
let strangers see by the cut of his robes that he was a
scholar, 1In the course of his speech the bigot Provost

~ said that tho owner of the Belfast News Letter is an

i puldip 001D CILE LEABA & CUbAID AN MIUSCINIM A, of Trinity College! One might have suspected so
: ~ Tuch concerning the owner of one of the most bitterly

anti-Irish and anti-Catholic papers in the country.
The Lord Mayor of Dublin, Mr. Nannetti, gave the
British and West British Institute of Journalists a
cead male failte, and Mr, T. M, Harrington, M.P., of
the Shamrock, was present ab the proceedings at the
City Hall. At various churches on Sunday sermons were

“preached having reference to the Journalists’ Confer-
ence. We note that the  Irish *’ Times of the imported
editor has a nice sense of precedence in matters of sects
and the Church. It published reports of four sermons
in quite interesting order. The first report dealt with
the sermon preached to the members of tho disestab-
lished, but not disendowed, sect, which is mostly com-
posed of England’s Faithful Garrison in this country;

- the second report was of the sermon delivered to mem-

bers of the Presbyterian sect; the third, that of the
sermon to the Methodist sect, and ab the tail-end the
“Irish  Times that libelled Oanon M‘Inerney gave a
report of the sermon delivered to the members of the
Catholic Church.

It is pitiable to find that journalists in Ireland arc
apparently so lacking in self-reliance that they have not
a combine of their own, but, as we have said, the real
reason of that is, that the journalists in Ireland are
mostly West-Britons.  Ieavon knows, Ircland is
sorely in need of the services of real Irish-Ireland
journalists, and she has very few of them.

It ought to make us all very conceited, whether we
be British, West-British, or Irish-Ireland journalists,
to read so much about the Power of the Press these
days. May we take, as it were, the part of devil’s
advocate, and suggest to our readers that there is a
great deal of shallow platitude talked and written con-
cerning the power of the Press; very often the power
really rests with the power that can press the Press. In
Ireland the talk of the power of the Press is mostly
raimeis. Why, Mr. Bung has probably more than half
the Press in Ireland in his fist; Dan Tallon need only
press the Magic Button and scores of able editors will
declare themselves the must obedient servant of the
great Bung. It would be difficult to say whether the
Freman’s Journal was more afraid of a Bishop’s pas-
toral or of the theatres at which such abominations as
““ The Spring Chicken ’’ are produced, and which adver-
tise such things at so much an inch in its columns.
The power of the Press in Ireland, indeed! How
many papers in Ireland have not their mouths shut by
a railway free pass or a few advertisements? Mr. Bell,
of the Great Sourface Railway, no doubt thought he
had an easy thing in hand when we were expected to
keep saying nothing about the Railway in exchange for
advertisements ; however, we elected to keep our inde-
pendence, and we let the Railway keep its ads. Picture
the Power of some of the local papers in Ireland as they
endeavour, with their knees trembling, to please as
many as they can and offend as few as possible. Who
wields whatever power they possess? Is it the able
editors!

As far as we can analyse it the Press is largely rather
a symptom of the public state than a great cause
creating effects; the Press, of whose Power so much is
said, is licked into shape by scores of forces that it is
not big enough, frequently not honest enough, to
combat. But if the Power of the Press as a phrase
applied to Journalism in Ireland is mostly raimeis,
there is no doubt the Press could have power if it had
the head and, what is more important, the heart to
exercise it. Though we say it ourselves, and it would
be only mock modesty not to frankly admit what every-
one knows, there can be no doubt whatever about the
Power of the LEapER. Heavens! what men and things
we have made jump. During six years we have been,
amongst other useful works, pressing the Press, and those
who know the country now and knew it then can see
the trace of our fingers on the face even of the
Freeman’s Journal. The Press could have a power if
it dared. We have run an independent Press in the
face of a complex combination of enemies—Bung,
Bigots, windy politicians, much of the Press, and the
most “ powerful ”” portion of it too, and the rest of the
enemies that our readers know without our reminding
them—and here wo are to-day sound in ‘wind and limb,
fresh for another six years of if, if God spares us, at

made the people of Ireland more a single whole than is

Is it good, then, that these things should be undone,
that the process of constructing a nation—a process car-
ried on through weary years of suffering—should be re-
versed and liberty be purchased at tho price of disin-
tegration? To-day Ireland means something to every
true Irishman. It is more to him than Leinster or Mun-
ster or Connacht. He has suffered for Ircland, his for-
bears have died for it, and he will not readily exchange
the province for the nation. If the Bengalee fights
desperately to prevent the partition of his ancient native
land, how much more should wo strive for the national
ideal, and refuse to be reduced to three big countries.
The condition of Australia before federation was not
unlike that which Ireland would be under the
new arrangement. A population about the same as our
own was divided into a number of small States, each too
weak to bear the burden of national life. The position
was found intolerable, and in federation was found the
-only solution of the difficulty. The result in Ireland
would be the same. Leinster, Munster and Connacht
would be too large for counties, too small for States,
and economic disaster would be added to the violation of
national sentiment. Of course a scheme of federation
for Ireland such as that suggested by ‘“-Tmaal’’ some
months ago, but which does not seem to be part of the
present proposal, with four provincial governments sub-
ject to a central authority in Dublin, thus reproducing
the Australian federal arrangement, would be less objec-
tionable. The chief difficulty would be that in a small
country we should have, over and above local govern-
ment, three central governments superimposed:—(1)
The provincial ; (2) the government in Dublin; (3) the
gevernment, in London. Something of the sort, of
course, exists in Australia, but the control of the
London government counts for much less there than it
would probably do under the new arrangement in Ire-

N a former article in the Lraper I endeavoured to
I pourtray some of the conditions which obtain in
the Western States, and which concern the Irish emi-
grant. T would like to make a few more remarks on
the same topic if I may trespass further on your valu-
able space.

Notwithstanding the efforts of the Gaelic League and
other patriotic societies, the Registrar General states
that the mumber of Irish cmigrants landed in the
United States during the first five months of the pre-
sent, year exceeds the number for the corresponding
period of last year by two thousand. No doubt the in-
crease is to be attributed in part to tho large number of
emigration agencies now established all ovor the coun-
‘try.  Yet the soi-disant “ National ”’ newspapers are
silent if not actually helping on the work of nation-
killing by accopting advertisements from steamship
companies. The politicians, who have spent some time
in the States and must be familiar with conditions here,
instead of telling the truth, paint a bright picture of the
wealth and power of their countrymen in America, and
of a “greater Ireland beyond the seas,”’” which exists
only in their imaginations. They cannot be blind to
the fact that every emigrant is as completely lost to his
country as if he had nover been born. Wealthy and
powerful many of the Irish in America are undoubtedly,

is wanting in the séntiment of love for ?hc old land, he

tered through the States are numbers of small towns

grant. I leave the explanation to the reader, and he
will get an idea of the immensity of the task undertaken

van of that mighty industrial force which has made

virile race can find a suitable field for the display of its
best qualities only in a foreign land. What stronger con-
demnation of that system of government which makes

good instance of the blighting effects which inevitably

that if the sons of the well-to-do farmer or business
man cannot enter the learned or genteel professions
and become lawyers, doctors, ete., they think their only
resource is in emigration. They reserve all their ener-
gies for the United States, for, they seem to think, that
if expended in Iroland they are wasted. I lived in one

Another fact which the intending emigrant often fails
to consider, is the deadly effect of the climate on most
emigrants from the northern parts of Europe. It is
a pitiful spectacle to witness the change which a few
years’ residence in America makes in the physical con-
dition of numbers of European emigrants.  The grind-
ing toil of the smelters and sweatshops changes the big
kS : rosy-cheeked, light-hearted boy into a pale, emaciated,
o3 dried-up wreck, prematurely old and grave; his vision
of easily acquired wealth rudely dispelled, and the El
Dorado of his fond anticipation a land of grim and
plodding toil, of fierce competition, where brute force
predominates. If the reader thinks that I paint a
picture .darker than the reality, T would refer him to
the leading American magazines, in which he will learn,
on the hest authority, how completely the American
rcitizen is at the mercy of the trusts and combines.

T have written this article with the hope that the
intending emigrant may consider before he comes
whethor ho will better himself materially by so doing.
T would now suggest, and the suggestion is probably not
a new one, that, as an offset to the work of the emi-
gration agents, a campaign of enlightenment be syste-
matically conducted by the priests in their respective
parishes. Tt is, perhaps, customary to expect too much
from the priests, to look to them to lead and guido us in
our temporal as woll as spiritual troubles, but, owing
40 their perfect organization and their influence, bhgy
ave, ipso facto, bofter fitted than any qt]wr body to
.counteract the evil work of the emigration agents. I
" have often thought that if the priests of Ireland had
~ an idea of how many Irish emigrants cease all practice
- of their religion, and, in plain language, go to the
devil,” they would make emigration and its ovils the
xt of each Sunday’s sermon. A Jesuib priest of this

THE LEADER.

43

city, who has beon for years in the arduous mission-
work of the West and South-west, spoke to me so
strongly on this subject that I think some of '_1115 Te-
marks worth quoting., He says:— J s

“T am ashamed to say that, in this very city, to go
no farther, we have vast numbers of Irish men a.nfl
women who have not only ceased to practise their reli-
gion, but have become members of those secret societies
condemned by the Church. They become Freemasons,
Odd Fellois, ete. ; their children go a step further and
become Baptists and Methodists, because by so doing
they have, as we say here, more ‘pull.” You must re-
member that, out here, the Catholic religion is not a
money-making asset.  Imagine finding such names as
John O’Bricn and Pat Kelly on the register for the
Protestant churches. It is a heart-break to me to see
the Irish emigrants lose that faith that neither tyranny,
persecution, nor death could force them to abandon.”’

Some there are, both here and in Ireland, who think
that there is no use trying to stop emigration until we
get Home Rule. I do not think that emigration can be
reduced to normal proportions under complete British
regime, but I think it can be very materially checked
by our own exertions. Nothing will be gained by
waiting until the remedy arrives, for, meanwhile, the
nation is bleeding to death.  Whilst we wait, disease is
cating into the body, and so much of the vitality and
energy will be destroyed that recuperation may be im-
possible.

P. F. CrLoBEssY,
